abstract: Generating motion-controlled videos--where user-specified actions drive physically plausible scene dynamics under freely chosen viewpoints--demands two capabilities: (1) disentangled motion control, allowing users to separately control the object motion and adjust camera viewpoint; and (2) motion causality, ensuring that user-driven actions trigger coherent reactions from other objects rather than merely displacing pixels. Existing methods fall short on both fronts: they entangle camera and object motion into a single tracking signal and treat motion as kinematic displacement without modeling causal relationships between object motion. We introduce MoRight, a unified framework that addresses both limitations through disentangled motion modeling. Object motion is specified in a canonical static-view and transferred to an arbitrary target camera viewpoint via temporal cross-view attention, enabling disentangled camera and object control. We further decompose motion into active (user-driven) and passive (consequence) components, training the model to learn motion causality from data. At inference, users can either supply active motion and MoRight predicts consequences (forward reasoning), or specify desired passive outcomes and MoRight recovers plausible driving actions (inverse reasoning), all while freely adjusting the camera viewpoint. Experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in generation quality, motion controllability, and interaction awareness.

citations.fullCitation: T. Wan, A. Wang, B. Ai, B. Wen, C. Mao, C.-W. Xie, D. Chen, F. Yu, H. Zhao, J. Yang, et al. Wan: Open and advanced large-scale video generative models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.20314, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper introduces the Wan2.1-14B model, which serves as the foundational pretrained backbone for MoRight. The authors of MoRight explicitly state they build upon and fine-tune this large-scale video generation model, making it a critical dependency for their work.
citations.title: Wan: Open and advanced large-scale video generative models

citations.fullCitation: D. Geng, C. Herrmann, J. Hur, F. Cole, S. Zhang, T. Pfaff, T. Lopez-Guevara, C. Doersch, Y. Aytar, M. Rubinstein, C. Sun, O. Wang, A. Owens, and D. Sun. Motion prompting: Controlling video generation with motion trajectories. arXiv preprint arXiv:2412.02700, 2024.
citations.justification: This is a key prior work in trajectory-based video generation that MoRight directly compares against in its experiments. MoRight positions its own contributions, such as disentangled motion control and causality reasoning, as solutions to the limitations of existing methods like Motion Prompting, which entangle camera and object motion.
citations.title: Motion prompting: Controlling video generation with motion trajectories

citations.fullCitation: R. Chu, Y. He, Z. Chen, S. Zhang, X. Xu, B. Xia, D. Wang, H. Yi, X. Liu, H. Zhao, et al. Wan-move: Motion-controllable video generation via latent trajectory guidance. arXiv preprint arXiv:2512.08765, 2025.
citations.justification: WanMove is another state-of-the-art motion-conditioned model that serves as a primary baseline for comparison in MoRight's evaluation. By benchmarking against WanMove, the paper demonstrates the superiority of its disentangled framework in terms of generation quality, motion controllability, and physical plausibility.
citations.title: Wan-move: Motion-controllable video generation via latent trajectory guidance

citations.fullCitation: J. Bai, M. Xia, X. Wang, Z. Yuan, X. Fu, Z. Liu, H. Hu, P. Wan, and D. Zhang. Syncammaster: Synchronizing multi-camera video generation from diverse viewpoints. Proc. ICLR, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper is cited as a direct inspiration for MoRight's core architectural choice. The authors explicitly credit this work for the dual-stream generation framework, which they adapt to decouple object motion (in a canonical view) from camera transformations (in a target view), a central contribution of their paper.
citations.title: Syncammaster: Synchronizing multi-camera video generation from diverse viewpoints

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "MoRight: Motion Control Done Right," focusing on its contributions to controllable video generation.

---

### Research Paper Analysis: MoRight: Motion Control Done Right

**1. Authors and Institution(s)**

The research was conducted by Shaowei Liu, Xuanchi Ren, Tianchang Shen, Huan Ling, Sanja Fidler, and Jun Gao from NVIDIA, in collaboration with Shaowei Liu, Saurabh Gupta, and Shenlong Wang from the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. The work by Shaowei Liu was performed during an internship at NVIDIA.

**2. How this work fits into the broader research landscape**

The work presented in "MoRight: Motion Control Done Right" is situated within the field of controllable video generation, a domain focused on producing dynamic visual content based on user-specified inputs. Existing methodologies in this area frequently encounter two primary limitations. First, they often entangle camera motion with object motion, typically by representing both as pixel-wise trajectories. This entanglement makes it challenging to control object movements independently of the camera viewpoint, as any change in camera perspective inherently alters all pixel trajectories. Second, current models often treat motion as a kinematic displacement, neglecting the underlying causal relationships between objects. This approach means models primarily focus on replicating specified trajectories rather than reasoning about how actions by one object might induce reactions from others. Consequently, generating physically plausible and interactive scenes often requires detailed, laborious, or privileged motion inputs, such as per-frame depth maps, 3D object trajectories, or foreground-background segmentation masks, or relies on external physics engines or large language models, which can introduce domain specificity or error propagation.

MoRight addresses these limitations by proposing a unified framework designed for disentangled camera-object motion control and motion causality reasoning. The paper positions itself as an advancement that allows users to specify object actions in a canonical view and adjust camera viewpoints independently, while the model internally learns and applies action-consequence relationships directly from data. This approach aims to bridge the gap toward interactive visual reasoning capabilities, which are considered foundational for applications in embodied AI, world models for physical simulation, and immersive content creation requiring flexible scene navigation and manipulation.

**3. Key objectives and motivation**

The central objective of the MoRight research is to develop a video generation system that facilitates controllable, interactive motion generation with an embedded understanding of motion causality. This overarching goal is pursued through several specific objectives:

1.  **Disentangled Camera-Object Motion Control:** A primary objective is to enable users to control the motion of objects independently from the camera viewpoint. This means allowing users to define object trajectories in a stable, canonical view while simultaneously specifying arbitrary camera movements (e.g., zoom, pan, orbit) to explore the scene. The motivation for this objective stems from the inherent entanglement of camera and object motion in pixel-space trajectory representations used by many existing methods, which makes joint control ambiguous and difficult for users.
2.  **Motion Causality Reasoning:** The project aims to empower video generation models with the ability to reason about cause-and-effect relationships between actions and their consequences within a scene. This goes beyond merely displacing pixels, ensuring that user-driven actions elicit coherent and physically plausible reactions from other objects. The motivation here is that real-world interactions are causal (e.g., pushing a cup causes it to slide and possibly collide). Specifying the motion of every affected object explicitly is impractical for users, necessitating a model that can infer these interactions.
3.  **Forward Reasoning (Action → Reaction):** MoRight seeks to allow users to input an "active" motion (a user-driven action) and have the model predict and generate the resulting "passive" consequences and the overall scene evolution.
4.  **Inverse Reasoning (Reaction → Action):** Complementing forward reasoning, an objective is to enable users to specify a desired "passive" outcome or trajectory for an object, and then have the model infer and generate the plausible "active" actions that would lead to that outcome.
5.  **Unified Framework Operation from a Single Image:** The system is designed to achieve these objectives starting from a single input image, generating a video that incorporates both user-defined motion and camera viewpoint adjustments while maintaining visual coherence and causal consistency. This provides a more flexible and practical interface for content creation compared to methods requiring extensive inputs or pre-computed 3D information.

The motivation for these objectives is rooted in the current limitations of video generation models, which lack the unified capability of interactive visual reasoning akin to human interaction with the physical world. Addressing these gaps is considered crucial for advancing applications such as embodied AI agents that need to anticipate action outcomes, world models that simulate physical interactions, and immersive content creation tools where users desire intuitive scene manipulation and navigation.

**4. Methodology and approach**

MoRight is constructed as a unified framework for video generation, utilizing a Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based latent video diffusion model. Its core methodology involves a dual-stream generation architecture for disentangled camera-object control and a motion causality modeling component based on motion decomposition and dropout.

**4.1. Disentangled Camera-Object Motion Control**
The approach for disentangling camera and object motion is founded on the insight that object motion is inherently unambiguous when expressed under a canonical static camera view. To implement this, MoRight employs a dual-stream generation framework:

*   **Dual-Stream Generation:**
    *   **Canonical Stream:** This stream is dedicated to modeling and generating object motion in the source image plane under a fixed, static canonical viewpoint. Users provide object motion inputs, such as pixel trajectories (𝜏_can_i), to this stream.
    *   **Target Stream:** This stream is responsible for generating the final video, incorporating both object motion and user-specified target camera motion (represented by camera poses {C_i}). Initially, this stream receives an empty object motion condition.
    *   **Shared Weights and Interaction:** Both streams share the same DiT weights but differ in their input conditioning and positional embeddings. Crucially, they interact through self-attention layers within each transformer block. This interaction facilitates the transfer of motion cues learned in the canonical view to the target view, with the canonical stream serving as a "virtual" anchor for consistent motion control. The model is jointly denoised across both streams.

*   **Condition Injection and Motion Transfer:**
    *   **Camera Encoding:** For the target stream, target camera poses are used to warp the first input image, and this warped representation is encoded into a latent camera condition (z_cam) using the VAE encoder. For the canonical stream, an identity camera matrix is used for warping.
    *   **Motion Encoding:** Per-pixel trajectory maps, which represent the user-specified object motion in the canonical view, are encoded into an embedding (e_trk) via a lightweight encoder. For the target stream, the motion encoding is initially set to zero or empty.
    *   **Feature Fusion and Cross-View Self-Attention:** Both camera and motion encodings are linearly projected and added to the latent features (f_i) at each transformer block (Equation 2). Subsequently, the features from both streams are concatenated and processed through a self-attention layer (Equation 3). This mechanism allows tokens from the target view to attend to motion-conditioned canonical tokens, enabling the implicit transfer of motion information across different camera viewpoints. This process of injection and synchronization repeats at every block, progressively transferring motion across views.

**4.2. Motion Causality Modeling**
To enable the model to reason about action-consequence relationships, MoRight decomposes object motion and employs a specific training strategy:

*   **Motion Decomposition:** All foreground object motion tracks (𝒯) are categorized into two components: active motion (𝜏_act), representing user-driven actions, and passive motion (𝜏_pas), representing the causal outcomes or reactions of other objects.
*   **Motion Dropout Training:** During training, a key mechanism is motion dropout. The model is randomly conditioned on either the active motion component or the passive motion component of the full ground-truth video (Equation 4). However, it is always supervised on the complete video containing both active and passive motions. This asymmetric supervision encourages the model to infer the missing motion component and internalize the causal relationship between actions and their consequences.
*   **Inference Capabilities:** This learned causality allows for two inference modes:
    *   **Forward Reasoning:** Users provide active motion inputs, and the model predicts and generates the resulting passive consequences and scene dynamics.
    *   **Inverse Reasoning:** Users specify desired passive outcomes, and the model recovers plausible active actions that would lead to those outcomes.

**4.3. Training Data Curation**
A multi-stage data curation pipeline (Fig. 4) is designed to acquire the necessary paired data for dual-stream training and motion decomposition:

*   **Motion Extraction and Canonicalization:** Raw videos are processed using foundation models (e.g., ViPE for depth and camera poses, AllTracker for dense pixel trajectories). These trajectories are then unprojected to 3D and reprojected into the first frame's canonical view (Equation 5) to establish a static reference for object motion.
*   **Active and Passive Motion Decomposition:** A vision-language model (Qwen3) is used to identify active and passive objects within the video, followed by instance segmentation (SAM2) to generate masks. Trajectories are then assigned to either active or passive components. Video captions are also rewritten to describe only one motion component for specific training conditions.
*   **Synthetic Data Generation for Paired Two-View Videos:** Videos with nearly static cameras are identified from collected datasets. Corresponding dynamic-camera videos are then synthesized using a camera-controlled video-to-video model, providing paired supervision for the canonical and target streams. Data augmentation, including basic camera operations, is used to increase camera diversity.
*   **Single-View Real-World Data for Mixed-Training:** To improve robustness and generalization, abundant single-view real-world videos are leveraged:
    *   Static-camera videos are duplicated to act as both streams, enabling the model to learn motion transfer from a motion-conditioned stream to a non-motion-conditioned stream.
    *   Videos with both camera and object motion are used to supervise only the target stream, providing realistic exposure.
*   **Rendered Graphics Data:** Synthetic data from sources like SyncCamMaster are integrated to further expose the model to diverse camera motions.

**4.4. Training and Inference Details**
The DiT is trained using a flow matching loss. Two dropout strategies enhance robustness and causal reasoning:

*   **Multi-granularity motion dropout:** During training, either fine-grained per-pixel trajectories or coarse, object-averaged trajectories are randomly selected as input, promoting capture of different levels of control.
*   **Occlusion and track dropout:** A subset of trajectories is randomly masked to simulate real-world occlusion and tracking failures, improving model robustness.
At inference, users specify object motion via sparse trajectories (drawing strokes or curves) on the first image, along with optional text prompts and target camera poses. Occlusion-aware masking is applied. The model jointly denoises both streams, and the output from the target stream is presented.

**5. Main findings and results**

The evaluation of MoRight was conducted across three datasets: DynPose-100K, WISA, and a custom Cooking benchmark, encompassing diverse interaction scenarios. The performance was assessed using a range of metrics covering video quality, camera accuracy, motion accuracy, and motion realism.

**5.1. Disentangled Camera-Object Motion Control**
MoRight’s performance in disentangled camera-object motion control was compared against several state-of-the-art controllable video generation methods, including Wan2.1 (base model), Gen3C (camera control only), and motion-conditioned models like Motion Prompting (MP), ATI, and WanMove. A key distinction is that MoRight utilizes only reprojected first-frame trajectories and camera poses as input, while baselines like MP, ATI, and WanMove often rely on privileged foreground/background tracks of all pixel trajectories across the video.

*   **DynPose-100K:** On DynPose-100K, a dataset characterized by dynamic camera motion, MoRight achieved comparable visual quality (PSNR, SSIM) and demonstrated strong motion accuracy (EPE), even though it did not surpass the top-performing WanMove in all metrics. The slight discrepancies were attributed to challenges with highly dynamic camera motion leading to potential errors in camera pose estimation and trajectory reprojection, which can degrade input signals.
*   **Cooking Benchmark:** On the Cooking benchmark, which features complex hand-object interactions, MoRight achieved the best overall performance in both visual quality (PSNR, SSIM) and motion control accuracy (EPE, Rotation/Translation error).
*   **Observation:** The study observed that baseline methods, which couple camera and object motion, sometimes prioritized the dominant motion mode in dynamic settings, potentially sacrificing either camera accuracy or object tracking fidelity. MoRight's disentangled formulation maintained more consistent object dynamics under varying camera viewpoints (Figure 5, 14).

**5.2. Motion Causality Modeling**
The capability for motion causality reasoning was evaluated on the WISA and Cooking datasets, focusing on generation quality (FID, FVD) and motion realism (Physical Commonsense - PC, Semantic Adherence - SA). MoRight was compared against MP, ATI, and WanMove. Unlike baselines, which typically receive detailed motion captions including full interaction descriptions, MoRight was provided with only a single active motion description, necessitating the model to infer passive outcomes.

*   **Quantitative Results:** MoRight achieved the highest PC score on WISA, indicating a strong ability to generate physically commonsensical interactions. It also demonstrated superior video quality (best FID and FVD) on both WISA and Cooking datasets. While its SA score was slightly lower than baselines (due to its intentional lack of explicit passive motion descriptions in prompts), it remained comparable, suggesting semantic alignment without relying on prior knowledge of consequences.
*   **Reasoning Capabilities:** Qualitative examples (Figure 7, 13) demonstrated MoRight's ability to perform both forward reasoning (predicting plausible scene consequences from specified active motion) and inverse reasoning (inferring feasible active actions from desired passive outcomes).

**5.3. Human Perceptual Evaluation**
A human perceptual study involving 11 participants and 330 evaluations was conducted to assess generation quality across controllability, motion realism, and photorealism. Participants compared videos from MoRight, ATI, and WanMove.

*   **Preference:** MoRight was preferred in the majority of cases: 53.5% for controllability, 54.6% for motion realism, and 55.9% for photorealism. This significantly surpassed ATI and WanMove, which received preferences in the range of 17-25% across categories (Figure 8).
*   **Conclusion:** This indicates that, despite baselines having access to privileged 3D trajectories and full interaction information, MoRight's disentangled control and causal reasoning resulted in subjectively higher quality, more controllable, and physically plausible video generations.

**5.4. Ablation Studies**
Ablation studies on the Cooking dataset examined various architectural choices, causal reasoning, hybrid training, and motion input granularities.

*   **Model Design:** A naive cascaded pipeline (static camera generation followed by camera control) yielded inferior results due to error accumulation. Removing the fixed-view branch (canonical anchor) significantly degraded camera and tracking accuracy, underscoring its importance for disentanglement.
*   **Causal Reasoning:** Disabling active/passive motion decomposition during training increased FID/FVD and reduced PC, confirming the degradation of interaction quality without causal reasoning.
*   **Mixed Supervision:** Training solely on paired data slightly degraded camera accuracy, highlighting the benefit of mixed supervision with real-world single-view data for camera diversity.
*   **Input Conditions:** MoRight demonstrated stable performance regardless of whether coarse segment-level trajectories or fine-grained pixel tracks were used, and whether active or passive motion inputs were provided. This indicated robustness to different motion granularities and input types.

**5.5. Limitation Analysis**
The paper acknowledges several limitations (Figure 9):
*   **Incorrect Interaction Reasoning:** The model may sometimes produce physically implausible outcomes (e.g., objects merging).
*   **Unnatural Motion with Sparse Tracks:** Occlusion leading to temporally sparse input trajectories can result in unnatural or unreliable inferred motion.
*   **Physical Inconsistency:** Generated motions may violate physical consistency (e.g., objects disappearing).
*   **Hallucinated Content:** The model can occasionally hallucinate new content in later frames.
*   **Complex Camera Motion:** Difficulty modeling very complex or fast camera motions, where drastic changes in camera input can degrade predicted interaction dynamics.

**6. Significance and potential impact**

The MoRight framework addresses fundamental challenges in controllable video generation, offering advancements with implications for various interactive AI and content creation domains.

Firstly, its core contribution of **disentangled camera-object motion control** resolves a significant limitation of prior methods where camera and object movements were implicitly coupled within pixel-space trajectories. By introducing a dual-stream architecture with a canonical static view, MoRight provides a more intuitive and flexible interface for users, allowing them to independently specify object actions and camera viewpoints. This separation is crucial for applications that require free exploration and manipulation within a generated scene, such as virtual reality environments or interactive simulations.

Secondly, the integration of **motion causality reasoning** represents a step beyond kinematic animation. By learning action-consequence relationships through active/passive motion decomposition and dropout training, MoRight enables the generation of physically plausible and coherent scene dynamics. This capability means that user-driven actions can trigger meaningful reactions from other objects, reducing the need for exhaustive manual specification of all object movements. The demonstrated abilities of **forward reasoning** (predicting consequences from actions) and **inverse reasoning** (inferring actions from desired outcomes) open new paradigms for interactive video editing and simulation, where users can either "play out" a scenario or "work backward" from a desired state.

The **unified framework, operating from a single input image**, increases the practical applicability of MoRight. This reduces the input overhead compared to methods requiring extensive 3D information or full video sequences. The comprehensive data curation pipeline, which includes synthetic paired data generation and mixed training with real-world single-view data, contributes to the model's robustness and generalization across diverse scenarios, while mitigating the limitations of purely synthetic datasets.

The **significance** of MoRight lies in its potential to:
*   **Advance Embodied AI:** By providing a system that can simulate action outcomes and physical interactions, MoRight can serve as a component for training and testing embodied AI agents, allowing them to anticipate and plan actions in dynamic environments.
*   **Improve World Models:** The causal reasoning capabilities contribute to building more sophisticated and interactive world models that can simulate complex physical dynamics, moving beyond mere visual prediction to understanding underlying physical laws.
*   **Enhance Creative Content Generation:** For filmmakers, animators, and game developers, MoRight offers a tool for more controllable and realistic content creation. Users can design complex scenes with intuitive control over object interactions and camera perspectives, streamlining workflows for visual effects and immersive experiences.
*   **Facilitate Interactive Editing:** The forward and inverse reasoning modes offer powerful new ways to edit and manipulate video content, providing users with more semantic control over scene evolution.

While the identified limitations, such as occasional physically implausible outcomes or challenges with highly complex camera motions, suggest avenues for future research, MoRight represents a substantial contribution towards more interactive, controllable, and reasoning-aware video generation systems.
overview:
## Introduction: The Challenge of Controlled Motion

Controllable video generation is the art of producing dynamic visual content that strictly adheres to user-provided specifications, such as how an object should move or how the camera should rotate. While recent advancements in generative AI have made it possible to create stunning videos from simple text prompts, providing fine-grained control remains a significant hurdle. Existing models often struggle with two fundamental issues: entanglement and a lack of causal reasoning.

Entanglement occurs because most models represent all motion—whether it is a person walking or the camera zooming in—as a series of shifting pixels. When a user tries to control both simultaneously, the model becomes confused. For instance, if you specify that a car should move forward while the camera orbits around it, the pixel-wise trajectories for the car are constantly changing due to the camera's perspective. This makes it extremely difficult for a model to maintain the integrity of the object's motion while also respecting the camera's path.

Furthermore, current systems typically treat motion through the lens of "kinematics"—simply calculating where pixels should land in the next frame. They lack an internal understanding of "causality," or the cause-and-effect relationships between objects. In the real world, if a hand pushes a cup, the cup reacts by sliding. Requiring a user to manually draw the trajectory for every single object involved in an interaction is impractical. A truly intelligent video generator should be able to infer the reaction from the action.

"MoRight: Motion Control Done Right" addresses these gaps by introducing a unified framework for disentangled camera-object control and causal motion reasoning. By separating the "what" (object action) from the "where" (camera viewpoint) and teaching the model to predict consequences, MoRight enables a more intuitive and physically plausible form of video creation.

![MoRight Overview: The framework enables forward and inverse motion reasoning while maintaining independent control over camera and object dynamics.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.07348v1/x22.png)

## Disentangling Camera and Object Dynamics

To solve the entanglement problem, MoRight moves away from the standard practice of modeling motion in a single, perspective-dependent view. Instead, it employs a dual-stream architecture based on a Diffusion Transformer (DiT). This design logic is built on the premise that object motion is unambiguous when viewed from a fixed, "canonical" perspective.

### The Dual-Stream Architecture
The MoRight framework operates two parallel generation streams that share the same underlying neural network weights:

1.  **The Canonical Stream:** This stream serves as a virtual anchor. It models the motion of objects in the source image plane as if the camera were completely static. Users define their desired object trajectories in this stable view.
2.  **The Target Stream:** This stream is responsible for generating the final video. it incorporates the user's specific camera movements (e.g., pans, tilts, or zooms) to produce the desired cinematic effect.

Crucially, these two streams are not isolated. They interact through a mechanism called Cross-View Self-Attention. At every layer of the transformer, tokens from the target view "attend" to tokens from the canonical view. This allows the model to synchronize the object motion learned in the stable canonical view with the dynamic perspective of the target view.

### Condition Injection
The model receives instructions through different latent embeddings. For the target stream, the camera pose $C_i$ is used to warp the initial image, which is then encoded into a camera condition $z_{cam}$. For the canonical stream, the user's object trajectories are encoded into a motion embedding $e_{trk}$. The features $f_i$ at each transformer block are updated as follows:

$$
f_i = f_i + W_{cam} z_{cam} + W_{trk} e_{trk} \text{ at each block } i
$$

Following this injection, the features from both streams are concatenated and processed through the self-attention layer:

$$
[f^{can}, f^{tgt}] = \text{Self-Attn}([f^{can}, f^{tgt}])
$$

This ensures that the target stream inherits the correct object dynamics from the canonical stream while maintaining the perspective required by the user's camera path.

![Architecture of MoRight: The dual-stream DiT uses shared weights and cross-view attention to transfer motion cues from a canonical static view to a dynamic target view.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.07348v1/x32.png)

## Motion Causality: Beyond Pixel Displacement

The second major contribution of MoRight is its ability to reason about motion causality. Rather than just moving pixels from point A to point B, the model learns the relationship between an "active" agent (the cause) and a "passive" object (the effect).

### Active and Passive Decomposition
MoRight categorizes all foreground motion into two types:
*   **Active Motion ($\tau_{act}$):** This represents the user-driven action, such as a hand reaching out to grab a teapot.
*   **Passive Motion ($\tau_{pas}$):** This represents the resulting consequence, such as the teapot being lifted or liquid pouring out.

By training the model on these distinct categories, MoRight gains the ability to perform complex visual reasoning. This is achieved through a "motion dropout" strategy during training. The model is sometimes shown only the active motion and asked to generate the full scene, forcing it to "fill in" the passive reactions. At other times, it is shown the passive goal and must infer the active cause.

### Forward and Inverse Reasoning
This causal understanding enables two powerful inference modes:
*   **Forward Reasoning ($\tau_{act} \to \tau_{pas}$):** The user specifies an action (e.g., "push this box"). The model then predicts and generates how the box should slide, tumble, or collide with other objects.
*   **Inverse Reasoning ($\tau_{pas} \to \tau_{act}$):** The user specifies a desired outcome (e.g., "the soccer ball should move into the net"). The model then automatically generates the plausible action that causes it, such as a player's foot kicking the ball.

This leap from kinematics to causality means users no longer need to be expert animators. They can specify high-level intents, and the model handles the complex physical interactions.

![Causal Reasoning Examples: MoRight can predict consequences from actions (forward) and infer actions from desired outcomes (inverse).](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.07348v1/x250.png)

## Data Curation and Training Strategy

To train a model capable of such sophisticated reasoning, the researchers developed a multi-stage data curation pipeline. Standard video datasets are often "flat"—they lack the paired information needed for dual-stream learning or causal decomposition.

### The Pipeline
The pipeline consists of several specialized stages:
1.  **Motion Extraction and Canonicalization:** Using foundation models like ViPE for camera poses and AllTracker for pixel trajectories, raw videos are converted into a structured format. The trajectories are then reprojected into a static canonical view to create the training signals for the canonical stream:
    $$\tau_{can} = \text{Project}(\text{Unproject}(\tau_{pix}, \text{Depth}), C_0)$$
2.  **Causal Labeling:** A vision-language model (Qwen) identifies which objects in a video are "active" and which are "passive." Instance segmentation models like SAM2 then extract the specific masks and trajectories for each.
3.  **Synthetic Multi-View Generation:** Because paired videos of the same scene from different viewpoints are rare, the researchers used a camera-controlled video-to-video model to synthesize dynamic-camera versions of static-camera real-world footage. This provides the "ground truth" for how motion should look when the camera moves.

The model is trained using a flow matching loss, which optimizes the probability paths for the diffusion process. By mixing synthetic multi-view data with abundant real-world single-view data, the model learns both the geometric precision of camera control and the complex textures of real-world physics.

![Data Curation Pipeline: The three-stage process involves motion extraction, active-passive decomposition, and paired multi-view synthesis.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.07348v1/x254.png)

## Evaluation and Impact

MoRight was evaluated against several state-of-the-art benchmarks, including DynPose-100K (for camera control) and WISA (for object interaction). The results consistently showed that MoRight outperforms baseline methods that couple camera and object dynamics.

### Key Findings
*   **Superior Controllability:** In human perceptual studies, participants preferred MoRight's outputs over 53% of the time for controllability and motion realism. This is particularly impressive because MoRight uses less input information (only trajectories on the first frame) compared to baselines that often require trajectories for every frame.
*   **Physical Commonsense:** Quantitative metrics showed that MoRight produces videos with significantly higher "physical commonsense" scores. It avoids common artifacts where objects pass through each other or disappear into thin air during interactions.
*   **Generalization:** Despite being trained on specific datasets, MoRight demonstrates robust generalization to a wide variety of scenes, from complex cooking tasks to outdoor sports.

### Significance and Impact
The implications of MoRight extend beyond simple video editing. Its ability to disentangle camera and object motion makes it a prime candidate for **Embodied AI**, where agents need to simulate the outcomes of their actions before executing them. It also serves as a powerful **World Model**, providing a way to simulate physical interactions in a purely visual domain without the need for expensive physics engines.

For content creators, MoRight offers a new paradigm of "interaction-based" editing. Instead of meticulously keyframing every movement, a creator can simply "drag" an object and let the model handle the cinematic camera work and the causal reactions, drastically reducing the time required to produce high-quality dynamic content.

While limitations remain—such as occasional hallucinations in very complex scenes or difficulty with extremely fast camera transitions—MoRight represents a significant step toward video generation models that truly understand the physical and causal structure of the world they are creating.

keyInsights: Object motion is unambiguously defined and transferred across different camera viewpoints when initially processed in a canonical static view, acting as a 'virtual anchor' within a dual-stream architecture.

Decomposing object motion into active (user-driven) and passive (causal outcomes) components, combined with asymmetric supervision during training, enables the model to reason about and generate physically coherent action-consequence relationships.

A hybrid training approach, leveraging both synthetic paired data for disentangled control and abundant single-view real-world data, significantly improves the model's robustness, generalization, and diversity in camera motion handling.
originalProblem: Existing controllable video generation methods often entangle camera and object motion, making it challenging to control movements independently of the camera viewpoint.

Current models frequently treat motion as kinematic displacement, neglecting underlying causal relationships, which requires laborious detailed inputs or external physics engines for physically plausible interactions.

Generating interactive scenes typically demands extensive, privileged motion inputs (e.g., per-frame depth maps, 3D object trajectories) or relies on external models, increasing complexity and potential for error.
results: MoRight received superior human preference scores, with 53.5% for controllability, 54.6% for motion realism, and 55.9% for photorealism, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

The framework demonstrated strong motion accuracy (e.g., best EPE and Rotation/Translation error on the Cooking benchmark) and visual quality (best FID and FVD on WISA and Cooking datasets) while maintaining consistent object dynamics.

It achieved the highest Physical Commonsense score on WISA and successfully performed both forward (predicting consequences from actions) and inverse (inferring actions from desired outcomes) causal reasoning from sparse user inputs.
solution: A dual-stream Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture is employed, featuring a canonical stream for object motion in a static view and a target stream for the final video, interacting via shared weights and self-attention for disentangled camera-object control.

Motion causality modeling is integrated through active/passive motion decomposition and motion dropout training, enabling the model to infer missing motion components and learn action-consequence relationships.

A comprehensive data curation pipeline combines motion extraction, canonicalization, synthetic paired two-view video generation, and mixed training with real-world single-view data to enhance robustness and generalization.
summary.summary: MoRight, developed by researchers at NVIDIA and the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, introduces a Diffusion Transformer-based framework for controllable video generation. It achieves disentangled control of camera and object motion while integrating motion causality reasoning, allowing users to define object actions in a canonical view, independently adjust camera viewpoints, and infer physically plausible action-consequence relationships.

title: MoRight: Motion Control Done Right
